title: Observation of Altermagnetic Order Switching in Bulk MnTe by Polarized Neutron Diffraction
authors: Zheyuan Liu, Shinichiro Asai, Shingo Takahashi, Hiraku Saito, Taro Nakajima, Takatsugu Masuda
abstract: Altermagnetic order, characterized by the Néel vector, breaks time-reversal symmetry (TRS) even in the nonrelativistic limit. Although spin-polarized and anomalous transport phenomena emerge with this order, they are mutually compensated by TRS-connected antiphase domains with opposite Néel vectors. Here we employ polarized neutron diffraction to directly probe the altermagnetic order in MnTe. Pronounced nuclear-magnetic interference terms were observed, providing direct evidence of a net Néel vector in the bulk crystal. Moreover, a weak ferromagnetic moment (WFM), originating from relativistic spin-orbit coupling, was found to be coupled with the altermagnetic order. Both the altermagnetic order and the WFM can be switched by milli-Tesla-scale magnetic field cooling.
summary: This paper provides direct experimental evidence for the existence and controllability of altermagnetic order in bulk MnTe. By using polarized neutron diffraction, the authors show that the Néel vector can be manipulated with very small magnetic fields, which is a crucial step toward using altermagnets in spintronic devices.
main_problem: Determining if altermagnetic order in bulk MnTe can be directly observed and controlled by external magnetic fields in the absence of net magnetization.
main_result: The researchers demonstrated that the altermagnetic Néel vector and the associated weak ferromagnetic moment (WFM) can be switched using milli-Tesla-scale magnetic field cooling, providing direct evidence of a net Néel vector in the bulk.
method: Polarized Neutron Diffraction (PoND) in half-polarized mode was used to probe nuclear-magnetic interference terms, complemented by magnetic susceptibility and spontaneous magnetization measurements.
model_or_system: The study focuses on bulk MnTe, a prototype altermagnet with a centrosymmetric hexagonal lattice (space group P63/mmc) and an A-type antiferromagnetic ground state.
key_observables: Nuclear-magnetic interference (NMI) vector R, weak ferromagnetic moment (WFM), Néel vector (n), and magnetic susceptibility.
important_parameters: Néel temperature TN = 307 K, magnetic field scales in the milli-Tesla range, and the presence of spin-orbit coupling (SOC).
main_assumptions: The study assumes the WFM arises from an imbalance in antiphase domains rather than from domain walls.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the MnTe domain states and the NMI vector mechanism; Fig 2 illustrates Néel vector configurations and susceptibility/WFM measurements; Fig 3 presents the NMI vector components under different cooling conditions and their temperature dependence.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of altermagnetism, describes the experimental setup for polarized neutron diffraction, presents the observation of NMI as evidence for the Néel vector, demonstrates domain switching via field cooling, and discusses the origin of the weak ferromagnetic moment.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Six altermagnetic domain states schematically shown for MnTe. The columns show orientational domain states related by a C3 rotation, and the rows show antiphase domain states related by time reversal T . (b) Two antiphase domain states (upper panel) with Néel vectors n+ and n−parallel to the a∗-axis. The NMI vector R aligns with the Néel vector and thus changes sign between the antiphase domain states. Spins are canted due to the alternating averaged DM vector Dave, leading to a ﬁnite net moment mc. The canting angle is exaggerated for illustration. The spin conﬁguration in the lower panel is forbidden. Calculated structure factors of (c) nuclei, (d) spins, and (e) the scalar...

FIG. 2. Schematics of (a) a multi-domain state in zero ﬁeld, (b) a set of orientational domain states selected by an in-plane ﬁeld Hb, (c), (d) Sets of antiphase domain states selected by an out-of-plane ﬁeld Hc, and (e) a single-domain state selected by an oblique ﬁeld Ho. (f) Magnetic susceptibilities of MnTe along the c-axis (χc, green), along the b-axis after ZFC (χZFC b , yellow), and along the b-axis after 5 T FC (χFC b , blue). The diﬀerence ∆χb = χFC b −χZFC b is scaled up by a factor of 10 and shown in gray. (g) Spontaneous WFM along the c-axis, mspon c , in MnTe. The excitation ﬁeld during the measurement (Hexc) was zero. The data were collected upon warming. (h) Cooling-ﬁeld...

FIG. 3. Components of the NMI vector, (a) Ry and (b) Rz, measured after ZFC, OFC, and FC at the Bragg peaks (001), (101), (003), (103), (201), and (203) at 100 K. Gaussian ﬁts with areas ﬁxed to the calculated values are shown as solid lines. The ﬁeld in OFC setup was applied in the bc-plane as shown in Fig. 2(e), and the ﬁeld in FC setup was applied in the c-axis. (c) Temperature dependence of Rz at (101) after 2.5 mT FC. A power-law ﬁt is shown as a solid line. (d) Cooling-ﬁeld dependence of Rz at (101) at 100 K. (e) Mapping of ˆn after each cooling condition in the ab-plane. The diﬀerent orientations of ˆn in the FC and OFC setups result in opposite signs of Ry in (a) and Rz in (b).

FIG. 4. σM y (red) and σM z (blue) obtained using z-polarization after (a) ZFC, (b) OFC, (c) FC at 2.5 mT, and (d) FC at −2.5 mT at the pure magnetic Bragg peak (001) at 100 K. The diﬀerence σM y −σM z is scaled up by a factor of 10 and shown in gray. Gaussian ﬁts with areas ﬁxed to the calculated values are shown as solid lines.
